metadata.title: Monkey: Optimal Navigable Key-Value Store
metadata.source: Dayan 等 - 2017 - Monkey Optimal Navigable Key-Value Store.pdf
metadata.notes: 正文双语版；按阅读器规范移除了图表、图表说明、致谢、参考文献和附录。

sections.title: Abstract
sections.originalMarkdown: In this paper, we show that key-value stores backed by an LSM-tree exhibit an intrinsic trade-off between lookup cost, update cost, and main memory footprint, yet all existing designs expose a suboptimal and difficult-to-tune trade-off among these metrics. We pinpoint the problem to the fact that all modern key-value stores suboptimally co-tune the merge policy, the buffer size, and the Bloom filters' false positive rates in each level. We present Monkey, an LSM-based key-value store that strikes the optimal balance between the costs of updates and lookups with any given main memory budget. The insight is that worst-case lookup cost is proportional to the sum of the false positive rates of the Bloom filters across all levels of the LSM-tree. Contrary to state-of-the-art key-value stores that assign a fixed number of bits per element to all Bloom filters, Monkey allocates memory to filters across different levels so as to minimize this sum. We show analytically that Monkey reduces the asymptotic complexity of the worst-case lookup I/O cost, and we verify empirically using an implementation on top of LevelDB that Monkey reduces lookup latency by an increasing margin as the data volume grows (50%–80% for the data sizes we experimented with). Furthermore, we map the LSM-tree design space onto a closed-form model that enables co-tuning the merge policy, the buffer size, and the filters' false positive rates to trade among lookup cost, update cost, and/or main memory, depending on the workload (proportion of lookups and updates), the dataset (number and size of entries), and the underlying hardware (main memory available, disk vs. flash). We show how to use this model to answer what-if design questions about how changes in environmental parameters impact performance and how to adapt the various LSM-tree design elements accordingly.
sections.translationMarkdown: 本文表明，以 LSM 树为基础的键值存储在查询成本、更新成本和主存占用之间存在内在权衡，但所有现有设计在这些指标之间提供的权衡都是次优且难以调优的。我们将问题定位为：所有现代键值存储都以次优方式联合调节合并策略、缓冲区大小以及各层布隆过滤器的假阳性率。我们提出 Monkey——一种基于 LSM 的键值存储，它能在任意给定主存预算下，在更新成本和查询成本之间取得最优平衡。核心洞见是，最坏情况下的查询成本与 LSM 树所有层级上布隆过滤器假阳性率之和成正比。与为所有布隆过滤器分配固定每元素位数的最先进键值存储不同，Monkey 在不同层级的过滤器之间分配内存，以使这一总和最小。我们从分析上证明 Monkey 降低了最坏情况查询 I/O 成本的渐近复杂度，并通过构建在 LevelDB 之上的实现进行实证验证：随着数据量增长，Monkey 降低查询延迟的幅度不断扩大（在实验数据规模下为 50%–80%）。此外，我们将 LSM 树设计空间映射为闭式模型，使系统能够联合调节合并策略、缓冲区大小和过滤器假阳性率，并根据工作负载（查询与更新的比例）、数据集（条目数量与大小）以及底层硬件（可用主存、磁盘或闪存），在查询成本、更新成本和/或主存之间进行权衡。我们展示了如何利用该模型回答假设性设计问题，包括环境参数变化将如何影响性能，以及应如何相应调整 LSM 树的各项设计要素。

sections.title: 1. Introduction
sections.originalMarkdown:
**LSM-Based Key-Value Stores.** Modern key-value stores that maintain application data persistently typically use a Log-Structured Merge-tree (LSM-tree) [27] as their storage layer. In contrast to traditional storage paradigms that involve in-place updates to persistent storage, LSM-trees perform out-of-place updates, thereby enabling (1) high throughput for updates [29] and (2) good space efficiency, as data is stored compactly [14] and does not need free space at every node to allow in-place updates. They do so by buffering all updates in main memory, flushing the buffer to disk as a sorted run whenever it fills up, and organizing disk-resident runs into levels of increasing sizes. To bound the number of runs that a lookup probes to find a target key, runs of similar sizes (i.e., at the same level) are sort-merged and pushed to the next deeper level when the current one becomes full. To speed up point lookups, which are common in practice [6], every run has an associated Bloom filter in main memory that probabilistically allows a run to be skipped if it does not contain the target key. In addition, every run has a set of fence pointers in main memory that map values to disk pages of the run (effectively maintaining min-max information for each page) and thereby allow searching a run for a target key in a single I/O. This design is adopted in LevelDB [19] and BigTable [12] at Google, RocksDB [15] at Facebook, Cassandra [21], HBase [5] and Accumulo [3] at Apache, Voldemort [25] at LinkedIn, Dynamo [13] at Amazon, WiredTiger [34] at MongoDB, and bLSM [29] and cLSM [18] at Yahoo. Relational stores also support this design. MySQL can run on RocksDB using MyRocks [14], which prefixes keys to indicate their table and maps SQL commands to RocksDB commands. SQLite also changed its storage engine from a B-tree in SQLite3 to an LSM-tree in SQLite4 [31].

**The Problem: Suboptimal Design Tuning.** We closely investigate the LSM-tree design space and show that LSM-based key-value stores exhibit an intrinsic trade-off among lookup cost, update cost, and main memory footprint (as discussed for various data structures in [8, 7]). Existing systems strike a suboptimal trade-off among these metrics. Their default configurations are not tuned along the optimal Pareto curve, beyond which lookup cost cannot be improved without harming update cost and vice versa for a given main memory budget. As a result, they cannot maximize throughput for a given main memory budget and application workload.

We pinpoint the problem to how all LSM-based key-value stores suboptimally co-tune the core design choices: the merge policy, the size ratio between levels, the buffer size, and the Bloom filters' false positive rates.

First, existing designs assign the same false positive rate (i.e., number of bits per element) to every Bloom filter regardless of the size of its run. Our insight is that worst-case point lookup cost over the whole LSM-tree is proportional to the sum of the false positive rates of all filters. Assigning equal false positive rates to all filters does not minimize this sum. Maintaining the same false positive rate across all runs means larger runs have proportionally larger Bloom filters, whereas the I/O cost of probing any run is the same regardless of its size because in-memory fence pointers allow direct access to the relevant disk page. Thus, the runs at the largest level consume most of the overall filter memory budget without yielding a significant reduction in lookup cost.

Second, the relationship between the design knobs and performance is nonlinear, making it difficult to co-tune the LSM-tree design space. Available main memory can increase Bloom-filter accuracy or enlarge the LSM-tree buffer. A larger buffer improves lookup performance because there are fewer levels to probe and improves update performance because entries pass through fewer merge levels. It is unclear how to divide a fixed main memory budget among these structures. Lookup and update costs can also be traded by varying the size ratio between levels and switching the merge policy between leveling and tiering, which support one run per level and multiple runs per level, respectively. Again, predicting the impact of a change is difficult because of the complexity of the design space.

**The Solution: Monkey.** We map the LSM-tree design space, allowing us (1) to holistically tune critical design knobs and achieve performance along the Pareto curve, and (2) to navigate that curve accurately to find the best trade-off for an application. We introduce Monkey: Optimal Navigable Key-Value Store. Monkey reaches the Pareto curve through a novel analytical solution that minimizes lookup cost by allocating main memory among Bloom filters so as to minimize the sum of their false positive rates. The core idea is to set each Bloom filter's false positive rate proportional to the number of entries in its run, meaning the false positive rates for shallower levels decrease exponentially. Any amount of memory allocated to the filter of a larger run brings only a relatively minor benefit in lowering its false positive rate, while the same memory has a greater impact when used to reduce the false positive rate of a smaller run.

We show analytically that this allocation removes a factor of $O(L)$ from worst-case lookup cost, where $L$ is the number of LSM-tree levels. The false positive rates form a geometric series whose sum converges to a constant independent of $L$. The number of levels is

$$
O\left(\log\left(\frac{N\cdot E}{M_{\mathrm{buffer}}}\right)\right),
$$

where $N$ is the number of entries, $E$ is the entry size, and $M_{\mathrm{buffer}}$ is the buffer size. This has two important benefits: lookup cost scales better with the number and size of entries, and lookup cost is independent of buffer size, removing the contention in allocating main memory between filters and buffer. Monkey therefore scales better and is easier to tune.

The second key element is navigating the Pareto curve to find the optimal balance between lookup and update costs under a given main memory budget and application workload (the lookup-to-update ratio). We map the design space and environmental parameters that affect this balance, and capture worst-case lookup and update costs as concise closed-form expressions. Existing key-value stores expose many tuning knobs; Monkey is novel in allowing precise navigation with predictable results, making a key-value store installation easier to configure or adapt.

**Contributions.** Our contributions are as follows.

- In Section 3, we show that LSM-tree-backed key-value stores exhibit a navigable trade-off among lookup cost, update cost, and main memory footprint, yet state-of-the-art stores are not tuned along the optimal trade-off curve because they do not allocate main memory optimally among Bloom filters and the LSM-tree buffer.
- In Section 4, we introduce Monkey, which optimizes lookup cost by allocating main memory among Bloom filters to minimize the sum of their false positive rates. We show analytically that Monkey's filter tuning reduces the asymptotic complexity of lookup cost and removes the dependence of lookup cost on buffer size.
- We identify the tuning and environment parameters that determine worst-case performance and model worst-case lookup and update costs as closed-form expressions. We use these models to find the holistic tuning that maximizes throughput under a uniformly random workload with any lookup/update ratio and maximizes the lower bound on throughput for any other workload.
- We show how the model answers what-if design and environmental questions: if the main memory budget, workload read/write proportions, number or size of entries, or storage medium changes, how should the LSM-tree design adapt and what is the impact on performance?
- In Section 5, we evaluate Monkey using an implementation on top of LevelDB under a wide range of lookup patterns, including existing versus nonexistent keys and varying temporal locality. Monkey improves lookup latency by 50%–80% in these experiments.
sections.translationMarkdown:
**基于 LSM 的键值存储。** 持久保存应用数据的现代键值存储通常采用日志结构合并树（Log-Structured Merge-tree，LSM-tree）[27] 作为存储层。传统存储范式会在持久化存储中执行原地更新，而 LSM 树执行异地更新，由此实现：（1）较高的更新吞吐量 [29]；（2）良好的空间效率，因为数据以紧凑形式存储 [14]，不必在每个节点预留用于原地更新的空闲空间。其做法是将所有更新缓存在主存中；缓冲区填满时，将其作为有序游程（run）刷写到磁盘；并将磁盘驻留游程组织成大小逐层增长的若干层级。为了限制查询目标键时必须探测的游程数量，同一层中大小相近的游程会进行排序合并，并在当前层填满时被推入下一更深层。点查询在实践中很常见 [6]。为加速点查询，每个游程都在主存中配有一个布隆过滤器；如果目标键不在该游程中，过滤器便能以概率方式允许跳过它。此外，每个游程还有一组位于主存中的围栏指针，它们把值映射到游程的磁盘页（实际上为每个页维护最小—最大信息），因而只需一次 I/O 就能在游程中搜索目标键。Google 的 LevelDB [19] 和 BigTable [12]、Facebook 的 RocksDB [15]、Apache 的 Cassandra [21]、HBase [5] 与 Accumulo [3]、LinkedIn 的 Voldemort [25]、Amazon 的 Dynamo [13]、MongoDB 的 WiredTiger [34]，以及 Yahoo 的 bLSM [29] 和 cLSM [18] 都采用了这一设计。关系型存储也支持它：MySQL 可通过 MyRocks [14] 运行在 RocksDB 上，MyRocks 为键添加前缀以指明所属表，并将 SQL 命令映射为 RocksDB 命令；SQLite 也将 SQLite3 中的 B 树存储引擎改为 SQLite4 中的 LSM 树 [31]。

**问题：次优的设计调优。** 我们深入研究 LSM 树设计空间，并表明基于 LSM 的键值存储在查询成本、更新成本和主存占用之间存在内在权衡（各种数据结构中的类似现象见 [8, 7]）。现有系统在这些指标之间取得的是次优权衡。它们的默认配置并不位于最优帕累托曲线上；对于给定的主存预算，越过该曲线后就无法在不损害更新成本的前提下改善查询成本，反之亦然。因此，这些系统无法在给定主存预算和应用工作负载下最大化吞吐量。

我们将问题定位为：所有基于 LSM 的键值存储都以次优方式联合调节各项核心设计选择，即合并策略、层间大小比、缓冲区大小和布隆过滤器假阳性率。

首先，现有设计不考虑相应游程的大小，为每个布隆过滤器分配相同的假阳性率（即相同的每元素位数）。我们的洞见是，整个 LSM 树的最坏情况点查询成本与所有过滤器假阳性率之和成正比。为所有过滤器分配相等的假阳性率并不能使这个总和最小。保持所有游程的假阳性率相同，意味着较大游程的布隆过滤器也按比例更大；然而，不论游程大小如何，探测任一游程的 I/O 成本都相同，因为主存中的围栏指针允许直接访问相关磁盘页。因此，最大层中的游程消耗了过滤器总内存预算的大部分，却没有显著降低查询成本。

其次，设计旋钮与性能之间的关系是非线性的，因此难以联合调节 LSM 树设计空间。可用主存既可以提高布隆过滤器的准确率，也可以扩大 LSM 树缓冲区。更大的缓冲区会减少需要探测的层数，从而改善查询性能；它还会减少条目必须经过的合并层数，从而改善更新性能。固定主存预算应如何在这些结构之间分配并不明确。还可以通过改变层间大小比，以及在分层（leveling）和分级（tiering）合并策略之间切换来权衡查询与更新成本；这两种策略分别允许每层一个游程和每层多个游程。由于设计空间复杂，变化带来的影响同样难以预测。

**解决方案：Monkey。** 我们映射了 LSM 树设计空间，由此能够：（1）整体调节关键设计旋钮，使性能达到帕累托曲线；（2）准确导航该曲线，为具体应用找到最佳权衡。我们提出 Monkey：最优可导航键值存储。Monkey 通过一种新的解析解到达帕累托曲线：在布隆过滤器之间分配主存，使其假阳性率总和最小，从而最小化查询成本。核心思想是让每个布隆过滤器的假阳性率与相应游程中的条目数成正比，也就是说，较浅层的假阳性率按指数下降。分配给大型游程过滤器的任意数量内存，在降低其假阳性率方面只能带来相对较小的收益；同样的内存若用于较小游程，则能更显著地降低假阳性率。

我们从分析上证明，这种分配从最坏情况查询成本中消除了一个 $O(L)$ 因子，其中 $L$ 为 LSM 树层数。各层假阳性率构成几何级数，其总和收敛于一个与 $L$ 无关的常数。层数为

$$
O\left(\log\left(\frac{N\cdot E}{M_{\mathrm{buffer}}}\right)\right),
$$

其中 $N$ 为条目数，$E$ 为条目大小，$M_{\mathrm{buffer}}$ 为缓冲区大小。这带来两项重要优势：查询成本随条目数量和大小扩展得更好；查询成本与缓冲区大小无关，从而消除了过滤器与缓冲区之间主存分配的争用。因此，Monkey 的可扩展性更好，也更容易调优。

第二个关键要素是在给定主存预算和应用工作负载（查询/更新比）的情况下，导航帕累托曲线以找到查询成本与更新成本之间的最优平衡。我们映射影响这一平衡的设计空间与环境参数，并将最坏情况查询和更新成本表示成简洁的闭式表达式。现有键值存储虽暴露了大量调优旋钮，Monkey 的新颖之处却在于能以可预测的结果精确导航设计空间，使键值存储的部署更容易配置或适配。

**贡献。** 本文贡献如下。

- 第 3 节表明，以 LSM 树为基础的键值存储在查询成本、更新成本和主存占用之间存在可导航的权衡；但最先进的存储没有位于最优权衡曲线上，因为它们没有在布隆过滤器与 LSM 树缓冲区之间最优分配主存。
- 第 4 节提出 Monkey。它在布隆过滤器之间分配主存，使假阳性率之和最小，从而优化查询成本。我们从分析上证明，Monkey 的过滤器调优降低了查询成本的渐近复杂度，并消除了查询成本对缓冲区大小的依赖。
- 我们找出决定最坏情况性能的调优参数和环境参数，并将最坏情况查询、更新成本建模为闭式表达式。借助这些模型，可以找到整体调优方案：对任意查询/更新比例的均匀随机工作负载最大化吞吐量，并对其他工作负载最大化吞吐量下界。
- 我们展示如何利用模型回答设计和环境方面的假设性问题：如果主存预算、工作负载的读写比例、条目数量或大小、存储介质发生变化，LSM 树设计应如何调整，性能又会受到什么影响？
- 第 5 节使用构建在 LevelDB 之上的实现评估 Monkey，覆盖多种查询模式，包括查询存在或不存在的键，以及变化的时间局部性。Monkey 在这些实验中将查询延迟降低了 50%–80%。

sections.title: 2. Background
sections.originalMarkdown:
This section provides the necessary background on LSM-trees. An LSM-tree stores key-value pairs. A key identifies an application object and allows its value to be retrieved. Both keys and values are stored inside the LSM-tree in this paper, although large values may also be stored outside the tree and referenced through fixed-width pointers attached to keys [26].

**Buffering Updates.** An LSM-tree conceptually consists of $L$ levels. Level 0 is an in-memory buffer; the remaining levels hold data in secondary storage. It optimizes inserts, updates, and deletes (collectively called updates) by storing them immediately in the Level 0 buffer without accessing secondary storage. Each entry carries a flag for deletion. If an update refers to a key already in the buffer, the original entry is replaced in place and only the newest survives. When the buffer reaches capacity, its entries are sorted by key into an array and flushed to Level 1 in secondary storage. We call these arrays runs.

Let $M_{\mathrm{buffer}}$ be the number of bits of main memory allocated to the buffer, defined as $M_{\mathrm{buffer}}=P\cdot B\cdot E$, where $B$ is the number of entries in a disk page, $P$ is the number of disk pages of main memory allocated to the buffer, and $E$ is the average entry size. LevelDB's default buffer size, for example, is 2 MB. Runs at Level 1 and higher are immutable. Level $i$ has a capacity threshold of $B\cdot P\cdot T^i$ entries, where $T$ is the size ratio between adjacent levels. Levels therefore have exponentially increasing capacities. The number of levels is

$$
L=\left\lceil \log_T\left(\frac{N\cdot E}{M_{\mathrm{buffer}}}\cdot\frac{T-1}{T}\right)\right\rceil. \tag{1}
$$

The size ratio has a limiting value $T_{\mathrm{lim}}=N\cdot E/M_{\mathrm{buffer}}$. As $T$ approaches $T_{\mathrm{lim}}$, $L$ approaches 1.

**Merge Operations.** To bound the number of runs a lookup must probe, an LSM-tree organizes runs by size and merges runs of similar sizes within a level. Two merge policies are possible: leveling and tiering [20]. Leveling favors lookups, while tiering favors updates. With leveling, Level $i$ contains at most one run, and a run moved from Level $i-1$ is immediately sort-merged with an existing run at Level $i$. With tiering, as many as $T$ runs can accumulate at Level $i$, at which point they are sort-merged. A leveled tree merges more greedily and therefore gives a tighter bound on the number of runs a lookup probes, at the cost of a higher amortized update cost. If multiple runs being merged contain the same key, only the entry from the youngest run is kept. The resulting run may therefore be smaller than the sum of the original runs. When a merge finishes, the resulting run moves to Level $i+1$ if Level $i$ is at capacity.

**Lookups.** A point lookup begins at the buffer and traverses levels from lowest to highest, and within a tiered level traverses runs from youngest to oldest. It terminates on the first matching entry because duplicate entries in older runs are superseded. A zero-result lookup, in which the key does not exist, may be expensive because it probes every run. A range lookup must sort-merge all runs with an overlapping key range to identify and ignore superseded entries.

**Probing a Run.** The original 1996 LSM-tree design [27] structures each run as a compact B-tree. As main memory became cheaper, modern designs began storing a flat array of fence pointers in memory, with min/max information for every disk page in every run [15, 19]. This is simpler and provides good in-memory binary-search performance. Because the tree is on disk, these searches are not in the performance-critical path; I/O is. A point lookup first searches the fence pointers, then reads the appropriate disk page with one I/O. A range lookup begins a scan from that page. Fence pointers have a modest footprint: with 16 KB disk pages and 4-byte pointers, they are about four orders of magnitude smaller than the raw data. We denote their memory footprint by $M_{\mathrm{pointers}}$ and assume it is $O(N/B)$, guaranteeing that probing a run takes $O(1)$ disk I/O for point lookups.

**Bloom Filters.** Every run has an in-memory Bloom filter [10]. A point lookup probes the filter before accessing the run. A negative result proves that the target key is absent and saves one I/O. A positive result means the key may exist, so the lookup probes the run. If it is absent, the result was a false positive and the lookup continues. The false positive rate (FPR) depends on the run's number of entries and the number of bits allocated to its filter:

$$
\mathrm{FPR}=e^{-\frac{\mathrm{bits}}{\mathrm{entries}}\ln(2)^2}. \tag{2}
$$

This assumes the optimal number of hash functions, $(\mathrm{bits}/\mathrm{entries})\ln(2)$ [32]. To the best of our knowledge, all LSM-based stores use the same bits per entry for all filters. A lookup therefore probes on average $O(e^{-M_{\mathrm{filters}}/N})$ of the runs, where $M_{\mathrm{filters}}$ is the total filter memory. Known implementations use 10 bits per entry by default [3, 4, 5, 15, 19, 29], corresponding to an FPR of about 1%. With an average entry size of 128 bytes, the filters are about two orders of magnitude smaller than the raw data.

**Cost Analysis.** We measure updates by amortized worst-case I/O cost, accounting for all merge operations an entry participates in after an update. For lookups, we measure zero-result average worst-case cost: the expected I/Os for a key not present in the tree. Zero-result lookups are common [11, 29] and incur the maximum pure I/O overhead. Unless otherwise stated, “worst-case lookups” and “worst-case updates” use these definitions.

For a tiered tree, worst-case lookup cost is $O(L\cdot T\cdot e^{-M_{\mathrm{filters}}/N})$ I/Os: there are $O(L)$ levels, $O(T)$ runs per level, each run probe costs one I/O, and only $O(e^{-M_{\mathrm{filters}}/N})$ of runs are probed on average. Worst-case update cost is $O(L/B)$ I/Os because every entry participates in one merge per level, and copying an entry costs $O(1/B)$ as each write moves $O(B)$ entries. For a leveled tree, worst-case lookup cost is $O(L\cdot e^{-M_{\mathrm{filters}}/N})$ because each level has one run. Worst-case update cost is $O(T\cdot L/B)$ because each update is copied $O(T)$ times per level through $O(L)$ levels.
sections.translationMarkdown:
本节介绍理解 LSM 树所需的背景知识。LSM 树存储键值对。键标识一个应用对象，并允许取回其值。本文讨论中，键和值都存储在 LSM 树内部；也可以把大值存储在树外，并通过附着在键上的定宽指针引用它们 [26]。

**缓冲更新。** 从概念上讲，LSM 树由 $L$ 层组成。第 0 层是内存缓冲区，其余层在辅助存储中保存数据。它把插入、更新和删除（以下统称更新）立即存入第 0 层缓冲区，无需访问辅助存储，从而优化这些操作。每个条目携带一个删除标志。如果更新引用缓冲区中已有的键，则原条目被原地替换，只有最新版本保留下来。缓冲区达到容量上限时，其中条目按键排序为数组并刷写到辅助存储的第 1 层。我们把这种数组称为游程（run）。

令 $M_{\mathrm{buffer}}$ 表示分配给缓冲区的主存位数，定义为 $M_{\mathrm{buffer}}=P\cdot B\cdot E$，其中 $B$ 为一个磁盘页可容纳的条目数，$P$ 为按磁盘页计量、分配给缓冲区的主存量，$E$ 为平均条目大小。例如，LevelDB 的默认缓冲区大小为 2 MB。第 1 层及以上的游程不可变。第 $i$ 层的容量阈值为 $B\cdot P\cdot T^i$ 个条目，其中 $T$ 是相邻层之间的大小比。因此，层容量按指数增长。层数为

$$
L=\left\lceil \log_T\left(\frac{N\cdot E}{M_{\mathrm{buffer}}}\cdot\frac{T-1}{T}\right)\right\rceil. \tag{1}
$$

大小比的极限值为 $T_{\mathrm{lim}}=N\cdot E/M_{\mathrm{buffer}}$。当 $T$ 接近 $T_{\mathrm{lim}}$ 时，$L$ 接近 1。

**合并操作。** 为限制一次查询必须探测的游程数量，LSM 树按大小组织游程，并合并同一层内大小相近的游程。合并策略有两种：分层（leveling）与分级（tiering）[20]。分层偏向优化查询，分级偏向优化更新。在分层策略下，第 $i$ 层最多包含一个游程；从第 $i-1$ 层移入的游程会立即与第 $i$ 层已有游程进行排序合并。在分级策略下，第 $i$ 层最多可累积 $T$ 个游程，达到该数量时才进行排序合并。分层树更积极地合并，因此能更严格地限制查询需要探测的游程总数，代价是更高的摊销更新成本。如果参与合并的多个游程含有同一键，只保留最年轻游程中的条目。因此，结果游程可能小于原游程大小之和。合并完成后，若第 $i$ 层已满，结果游程会移至第 $i+1$ 层。

**查询。** 点查询从缓冲区开始，由浅到深遍历各层；在分级层内部，则按从年轻到年老的顺序遍历游程。找到第一个匹配条目后即终止，因为较老游程中的同键条目已经过期。零结果查询所查键不存在，可能代价很高，因为它必须探测每个游程。范围查询必须对所有键范围相交的游程进行排序合并，以识别并忽略已被覆盖的条目。

**探测游程。** 1996 年提出的原始 LSM 树设计 [27] 将每个游程组织为紧凑 B 树。随着主存成本下降，现代设计改为在内存中保存扁平的围栏指针数组，其中含有每个游程每个磁盘页的最小/最大信息 [15, 19]。这种结构更简单，在内存中用二分搜索即可获得良好性能。由于树位于磁盘上，这些搜索并非性能关键路径，I/O 才是。点查询先搜索围栏指针，再用一次 I/O 读取适当的磁盘页；范围查询则从该页开始扫描。围栏指针占用很小：使用 16 KB 磁盘页和 4 字节指针时，它们比原始数据小约四个数量级。我们用 $M_{\mathrm{pointers}}$ 表示其内存占用，并假设它为 $O(N/B)$，从而保证点查询探测一个游程只需 $O(1)$ 次磁盘 I/O。

**布隆过滤器。** 每个游程都有一个内存中的布隆过滤器 [10]。点查询在访问游程之前先探测过滤器。否定结果能够证明目标键不存在，从而节省一次 I/O；肯定结果则表示键可能存在，因此查询要探测游程。若键实际不存在，该结果就是假阳性，查询继续进行。假阳性率（FPR）取决于游程的条目数和分配给过滤器的位数：

$$
\mathrm{FPR}=e^{-\frac{\mathrm{bits}}{\mathrm{entries}}\ln(2)^2}. \tag{2}
$$

该式假设采用最优哈希函数数量 $(\mathrm{bits}/\mathrm{entries})\ln(2)$ [32]。据我们所知，所有基于 LSM 的存储都为全部过滤器采用相同的每元素位数。因此，一次查询平均探测 $O(e^{-M_{\mathrm{filters}}/N})$ 比例的游程，其中 $M_{\mathrm{filters}}$ 是过滤器总内存。已知实现默认使用每元素 10 位 [3, 4, 5, 15, 19, 29]，对应约 1% 的 FPR。若平均条目大小为 128 字节，过滤器比原始数据小约两个数量级。

**成本分析。** 我们以摊销最坏情况 I/O 成本衡量更新，计入条目更新后参与的全部合并操作。查询则采用零结果平均最坏情况成本，即查找树中不存在的键时的期望 I/O 次数。零结果查询很常见 [11, 29]，并产生最大的纯 I/O 开销。除非另有说明，本文的“最坏情况查询”和“最坏情况更新”均采用这些定义。

对于分级树，最坏情况查询成本为 $O(L\cdot T\cdot e^{-M_{\mathrm{filters}}/N})$ 次 I/O：共有 $O(L)$ 层、每层 $O(T)$ 个游程、每次游程探测花费一次 I/O，且平均只探测 $O(e^{-M_{\mathrm{filters}}/N})$ 比例的游程。最坏情况更新成本为 $O(L/B)$ 次 I/O，因为每个条目在每层参与一次合并，而一次写 I/O 会移动 $O(B)$ 个条目，所以复制一个条目的成本为 $O(1/B)$。对于分层树，由于每层只有一个游程，最坏情况查询成本为 $O(L\cdot e^{-M_{\mathrm{filters}}/N})$；每次更新在每层被复制 $O(T)$ 次，并经过 $O(L)$ 层，因此最坏情况更新成本为 $O(T\cdot L/B)$。

sections.title: 3. LSM-Tree Design Space
sections.originalMarkdown:
This section describes critical trade-offs and tuning contentions in the LSM-tree design space. Our contributions are enabled by a detailed mapping of this space. We identify contentions among tuning knobs here and resolve them with Monkey in the next section.

Lookup and update costs depend on (1) the merge policy, tiering versus leveling; (2) size ratio $T$; (3) main-memory allocation between buffer $M_{\mathrm{buffer}}$ and filters $M_{\mathrm{filters}}$; and (4) allocation of $M_{\mathrm{filters}}$ among individual filters. The main observation is:

> The design space of LSM-trees spans everything between a write-optimized log and a read-optimized sorted array.

The question is how to navigate this space accurately and determine the exact impact of every design decision.

**Tuning the Merge Policy and Size Ratio.** When $T=2$, the lookup and update cost complexities of tiering and leveling are identical. Increasing $T$ under tiering decreases update cost and increases lookup cost; increasing it under leveling does the opposite. The two policies therefore provide complementary ways to navigate the same trade-off continuum. As $T$ approaches $T_{\mathrm{lim}}$, $L$ approaches 1. With one level, a tiered tree degenerates into a write-friendly log, while a leveled tree degenerates into a read-friendly sorted array. An LSM-tree can thus be tuned anywhere between these extremes.

**Tuning Main Memory Allocation.** The curve's limits are determined by memory allocation between filters and buffer. This allocation matters because DRAM costs about two orders of magnitude more per bit than disk and consumes about four times as much power per bit at runtime [24, 33]. The memory used by Bloom filters and the buffer is therefore a significant fraction of infrastructure and operating cost and should be used carefully.

**Design Space Contentions.** We identify three critical contentions. First, reallocating $M_{\mathrm{filters}}$ from one filter to another increases one FPR and decreases another; how should memory be allocated to minimize lookup cost? Second, more buffer memory reduces both lookup and update costs by reducing the number of levels, but it leaves less memory for accurate filters and can therefore increase lookup cost; how should a fixed memory budget be divided? Third, merge policy and size ratio must be tuned for a workload containing updates, zero-result lookups, non-zero-result lookups, and range lookups of varying selectivity. Decreasing $T$ under tiering or increasing it under leveling improves lookup cost and degrades update cost, but each operation type changes at a different rate. How can the best policy and ratio be found for an application?

**The State of the Art.** All known LSM-tree stores apply static and suboptimal choices to these contentions. Their Bloom filters are tuned equally, their buffer-to-filter size ratio is static, and their size ratio and merge policy are static [3, 4, 5, 15, 19, 29]. These aspects are orthogonal to other differences among the stores, including centralized versus decentralized architecture, consistency guarantees, and data models. Monkey improves on the state of the art by resolving these contentions and enabling quick, accurate, optimal navigation of the design space.
sections.translationMarkdown:
本节描述 LSM 树设计空间中的关键权衡与调优争用。本文的贡献建立在对这一空间的详细映射之上。这里先识别各调优旋钮之间的争用，下一节再用 Monkey 加以解决。

查询与更新成本取决于：（1）分级或分层合并策略；（2）大小比 $T$；（3）缓冲区 $M_{\mathrm{buffer}}$ 与过滤器 $M_{\mathrm{filters}}$ 之间的主存分配；（4）$M_{\mathrm{filters}}$ 在各个过滤器之间的分配。主要观察是：

> LSM 树的设计空间涵盖了从写优化日志到读优化有序数组之间的一切形态。

问题在于如何准确导航这一空间，并确定每项设计决策的确切影响。

**调节合并策略和大小比。** 当 $T=2$ 时，分级与分层的查询、更新成本复杂度相同。在分级策略下增大 $T$ 会降低更新成本、提高查询成本；在分层策略下增大 $T$ 则产生相反效果。因此，两种策略是导航同一权衡连续体的互补方式。当 $T$ 接近 $T_{\mathrm{lim}}$ 时，$L$ 接近 1。只有一层时，分级树退化为写友好的日志，分层树退化为读友好的有序数组。由此，LSM 树可以调节到两个极端之间的任意位置。

**调节主存分配。** 曲线的边界由过滤器与缓冲区之间的内存分配决定。这项分配十分重要，因为 DRAM 的每比特成本约为磁盘的两个数量级，每比特运行时功耗约为磁盘的四倍 [24, 33]。布隆过滤器与缓冲区使用的内存因而占据基础设施成本与运行成本的显著部分，应当谨慎利用。

**设计空间争用。** 我们识别出三项关键争用。第一，把 $M_{\mathrm{filters}}$ 从一个过滤器重新分配给另一个过滤器，会提高前者的 FPR、降低后者的 FPR；应如何分配内存才能最小化查询成本？第二，更多缓冲区内存通过减少层数来同时降低查询和更新成本，但这会留给高精度过滤器更少的内存，因而可能提高查询成本；应如何划分固定内存预算？第三，必须针对由更新、零结果查询、非零结果查询以及不同选择率的范围查询组成的工作负载，调节合并策略与大小比。在分级策略下降低 $T$ 或在分层策略下提高 $T$，都会改善查询成本、损害更新成本，但各操作类型的变化速率不同。应如何为应用找到最佳策略与大小比？

**现有技术。** 所有已知 LSM 树存储都对这些争用采用静态且次优的选择。它们以相同方式调节所有布隆过滤器，缓冲区与过滤器的大小比例固定，大小比和合并策略也固定 [3, 4, 5, 15, 19, 29]。这些方面与各存储之间的其他差异相互正交，例如集中式与分布式架构、一致性保证和数据模型。Monkey 通过解决这些争用并支持快速、准确、最优地导航设计空间，对现有技术作出改进。

sections.title: 4. Monkey
sections.originalMarkdown:
Monkey is an LSM-tree-based key-value store that can reach and navigate the Pareto curve to find the best possible balance between lookup and update costs for any main-memory budget, workload, and storage medium. It maximizes throughput for uniformly random workloads and maximizes the lower bound on throughput for all other workloads. Monkey does this by resolving contentions in the design space and using models to trade optimally among lookup cost, update cost, and main-memory footprint.

**Design Knobs.** Monkey turns the design elements that affect worst-case behavior into tuning knobs: (1) size ratio $T$ among levels, (2) merge policy (leveling or tiering), (3) false positive rates $p_1,\ldots,p_L$ assigned to Bloom filters at different levels, and (4) allocation of main memory $M$ between the buffer $M_{\mathrm{buffer}}$ and filters $M_{\mathrm{filters}}$. It can co-tune these knobs for throughput or favor a particular metric, such as an upper bound on average lookup or update latency.

**Minimizing Lookup Cost.** Monkey's first core element is optimal memory allocation across Bloom filters. Lookup cost is proportional to the sum of their false positive rates. Section 4.1 shows how to tune filters differently across levels to minimize this sum, yielding faster reads for any memory budget and shifting the entire trade-off curve down to the Pareto curve.

**Performance Prediction.** Monkey's second element is predicting how a change in a design choice or environmental parameter affects worst-case performance. Section 4.2 derives closed-form models for lookup and update costs in terms of the design-space knobs. The models predict, for example, how changing total main memory or its allocation repositions the Pareto curve. We derive an analogous model for the state of the art and show that Monkey dominates it.

**Autotuning.** Monkey's third element is holistic self-tuning to maximize worst-case throughput. Section 4.3 uses asymptotic analysis to map the space and derive a rule for allocating memory between buffer and filters. Section 4.4 models worst-case throughput from lookup and update costs, workload proportions, and persistent-storage read/write costs. An algorithm searches the Pareto curve for the balance that maximizes worst-case throughput.

Although we present Monkey as a key-value store, its results apply to any application that uses an LSM-tree.

### 4.1 Minimizing Lookup Cost

We now show how Monkey minimizes worst-case lookup cost. We initially focus on zero-result lookups because they incur the greatest pure I/O overhead. Minimizing their worst-case cost provides robust performance, and experiments later show significant gains for non-zero-result lookups and skewed workloads as well. Let $R$ denote the expected worst-case I/O cost of a zero-result point lookup. We first show that $R$ is the sum of all Bloom-filter FPRs, then tune those FPRs to minimize the sum subject to total filter memory $M_{\mathrm{filters}}$. This section assumes fixed entry size $E$; Appendix C of the original paper gives an iterative optimization algorithm for variable entry sizes.

**Modeling Average Worst-Case Lookup Cost.** The expected number of runs probed by a zero-result lookup is the sum of their filter FPRs. With leveling, each level contains at most one run. With tiering, each level contains at most $T-1$ runs; the arrival of the $T$th run triggers a merge and push. All runs at a tiered level share an FPR because they have the same size. Thus,

$$
R=
\begin{cases}
\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{L}p_i, & \text{leveling},\\[4pt]
(T-1)\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{L}p_i, & \text{tiering},
\end{cases}
\qquad 0<p_i\le 1. \tag{3}
$$

**Modeling Main-Memory Footprint.** Rearranging Equation (2) gives

$$
\mathrm{bits}=-\mathrm{entries}\cdot\frac{\ln(\mathrm{FPR})}{\ln(2)^2}.
$$

This equation applies to any number of filters with the same FPR, so it covers both one filter per level under leveling and $T-1$ under tiering. The last level contains at most $N(T-1)/T$ entries; Level $i$ contains at most $N(T-1)/(T\cdot T^{L-i})$. Summing over levels yields

$$
M_{\mathrm{filters}}=-\frac{N}{\ln(2)^2}\cdot\frac{T-1}{T}\sum_{i=1}^{L}\frac{\ln(p_i)}{T^{L-i}}. \tag{4}
$$

**Minimizing Lookup Cost with Monkey.** Equations (3) and (4) compute lookup cost and filter footprint for any assignment of $p_1,\ldots,p_L$. To minimize $R$ for a fixed $M_{\mathrm{filters}}$, the paper first solves the equivalent converse problem: minimizing $M_{\mathrm{filters}}$ for a specified $R$. Applying Lagrange multipliers gives the following optimal assignments, where $L=L_{\mathrm{filtered}}+L_{\mathrm{unfiltered}}$:

$$
p_i=
\begin{cases}
1, & i>L_{\mathrm{filtered}},\\[3pt]
\displaystyle\frac{(R-L_{\mathrm{unfiltered}})(T-1)}{T^{L_{\mathrm{filtered}}+1-i}}, & \text{otherwise},
\end{cases}
$$

for leveling, with $0<R\le L$ and
$L_{\mathrm{filtered}}=L-\max(0,\lfloor R-1\rfloor)$; and

$$
p_i=
\begin{cases}
1, & i>L_{\mathrm{filtered}},\\[3pt]
\displaystyle\frac{(R-L_{\mathrm{unfiltered}}(T-1))(T-1)}{T^{L_{\mathrm{filtered}}+1-i}}, & \text{otherwise},
\end{cases}
$$

for tiering, with $0<R\le L(T-1)$ and
$L_{\mathrm{filtered}}=L-\max(0,\lfloor(R-1)/(T-1)\rfloor)$ [Equations (5) and (6)].

The optimal FPR at Level $i$ is $T$ times the FPR at Level $i-1$: it is proportional to the number of elements in the level. Probing any run costs the same I/O regardless of run size, yet obtaining a low FPR at a deep level requires much more memory because the level has exponentially more entries. It is therefore better to allocate more bits per entry, and hence a lower FPR, to smaller levels. State-of-the-art stores instead assign the same FPR to all levels.

As the permitted lookup cost $R$ rises, less filter memory is required. The deepest filters eventually disappear as their optimal FPRs reach 1. Monkey minimizes memory for a desired $R$ by selecting the optimal number of filtered levels and setting the FPR at every filtered level proportional to its capacity.

### 4.2 Predicting Lookup and Update Costs

We next map the LSM-tree design space into closed-form lookup and update cost models and analytically show that Monkey dominates state-of-the-art designs.

**Zero-Result Lookup Cost ($R$).** Substituting the optimal FPRs into Equation (4) and rearranging gives

$$
R=R_{\mathrm{filtered}}+R_{\mathrm{unfiltered}}, \tag{7}
$$

where

$$
R_{\mathrm{filtered}}=
\begin{cases}
\displaystyle\frac{T^{T/(T-1)}}{T-1}\,e^{-\frac{M_{\mathrm{filters}}}{N}\ln(2)^2\,T^{L_{\mathrm{unfiltered}}}}, & \text{leveling},\\[7pt]
\displaystyle T^{T/(T-1)}e^{-\frac{M_{\mathrm{filters}}}{N}\ln(2)^2\,T^{L_{\mathrm{unfiltered}}}}, & \text{tiering},
\end{cases}
$$

and

$$
R_{\mathrm{unfiltered}}=
\begin{cases}
L_{\mathrm{unfiltered}}, & \text{leveling},\\
L_{\mathrm{unfiltered}}(T-1), & \text{tiering}.
\end{cases}
$$

The two additive terms are the expected runs probed in levels with and without filters. When $T=2$, tiering and leveling are identical and the expressions agree. The number of unfiltered levels is

$$
L_{\mathrm{unfiltered}}=
\begin{cases}
0, & M_{\mathrm{threshold}}\le M_{\mathrm{filters}},\\[3pt]
\left\lceil\log_T\left(\frac{M_{\mathrm{threshold}}}{M_{\mathrm{filters}}}\right)\right\rceil,
& \frac{M_{\mathrm{threshold}}}{T^L}\le M_{\mathrm{filters}}\le M_{\mathrm{threshold}},\\[7pt]
L, & 0\le M_{\mathrm{filters}}\le \frac{M_{\mathrm{threshold}}}{T^L},
\end{cases} \tag{8}
$$

with

$$
M_{\mathrm{threshold}}=\frac{N}{\ln(2)^2}\cdot\frac{\ln(T)}{T-1}.
$$

This threshold is the memory at which the last-level filter's FPR reaches 1.

**Non-Zero-Result Lookup Cost ($V$).** For the worst case in which the target key is in the oldest run, subtract the oldest filter's FPR $p_L$ from $R$ and add one I/O to read the matching page:

$$
V=R-p_L+1. \tag{9}
$$

**Update Cost ($W$).** Assume an entry is updated at most once per $N$ application writes, so no entry is eliminated before reaching the largest level. An entry participates in approximately $(T-1)/T$ merges per level under tiering and $(T-1)/2$ under leveling. Multiply by $L$, divide by $B$ entries per write I/O, and multiply by $(1+\phi)$ to account for reading source runs and for a persistent-storage write/read cost ratio of $\phi$:

$$
W=
\begin{cases}
\displaystyle\frac{L}{B}\cdot\frac{T-1}{2}\cdot(1+\phi), & \text{leveling},\\[7pt]
\displaystyle\frac{L}{B}\cdot\frac{T-1}{T}\cdot(1+\phi), & \text{tiering}.
\end{cases} \tag{10}
$$

When $T=2$, the two are identical.

**Range Lookup Cost ($Q$).** A range lookup makes $L$ disk seeks under leveling or $L(T-1)$ under tiering, followed by sequential scans. If the range includes a fraction $s$ of all entries, the cumulative scan is $sN/B$ pages:

$$
Q=
\begin{cases}
\displaystyle s\frac{N}{B}+L, & \text{leveling},\\[6pt]
\displaystyle s\frac{N}{B}+L(T-1), & \text{tiering}.
\end{cases} \tag{11}
$$

**Modeling the State of the Art.** The models for $V$, $W$, and $Q$ are unchanged because Monkey's core filter allocation does not alter these operations. The state-of-the-art zero-result model is obtained by setting all $p_i$ in Equation (3) equal; its full derivation appears in Appendix E of the original paper.

**Monkey Dominates Existing Designs.** Comparing Monkey's model with the state-of-the-art model shows that Monkey has lower lookup cost for every value of $M_{\mathrm{filters}}$, because it allocates the available memory optimally. As $M_{\mathrm{filters}}$ approaches zero, both degenerate to trees without filters and converge. Under tiering, both lookup-cost curves are stretched upward by $T-1$ because each level has $T-1$ runs.

### 4.3 Scalability and Tunability

Asymptotic analysis shows that Monkey scales better with data volume and removes lookup cost's dependence on buffer size.

**Complexity Analysis.** Worst-case lookup complexity differs according to whether $M_{\mathrm{filters}}$ is above or below $M_{\mathrm{threshold}}$. When it is above, all levels have filters, so $R_{\mathrm{unfiltered}}=0$ and $R_{\mathrm{filtered}}$ dominates. Substituting $M_{\mathrm{threshold}}$ into Equation (7) shows that $R_{\mathrm{filtered}}$ is at most $O(1)$ for leveling and $O(T)$ for tiering. As $N$ grows relative to filter memory, $M_{\mathrm{filters}}$ eventually falls below the threshold, at which point $R_{\mathrm{unfiltered}}$ becomes nonzero and dominates: it grows from at least $O(1)$ or $O(T)$ to $O(L)$ or $O(LT)$.

The condition $M_{\mathrm{filters}}>M_{\mathrm{threshold}}$ is equivalent to

$$
\frac{M_{\mathrm{filters}}}{N}>\frac{1}{\ln(2)^2}\cdot\frac{\ln(T)}{T-1}.
$$

The right side is at most 1.44 bits per element, attained at $T=2$. Modern stores typically use 10 bits per element, so in practice Monkey's lookup complexity is usually $O(R_{\mathrm{filtered}})$.

**Comparison with the State of the Art.** When $M_{\mathrm{filters}}\ge M_{\mathrm{threshold}}$, Monkey removes a factor of $O(L)$ from lookup complexity for both policies. Since

$$
L=O\left(\log_T\left(\frac{N\cdot E}{M_{\mathrm{buffer}}}\right)\right),
$$

Monkey's lookup cost is asymptotically independent of the number of levels. Its level FPRs decrease geometrically toward shallower levels, so the expected total filter-probe cost converges to a constant. This yields three benefits:

1. If the filter footprint scales with the number of entries, keeping $M_{\mathrm{filters}}/N$ fixed, Monkey's lookup cost remains fixed while the state of the art grows logarithmically. Monkey's advantage therefore increases with the dataset size.
2. Lookup cost is independent of entry size and does not increase for datasets with larger entries.
3. Lookup cost is independent of buffer size, simplifying memory tuning because the buffer and filters need not be carefully balanced to optimize lookup performance.

When $M_{\mathrm{filters}}\le M_{\mathrm{threshold}}$, state-of-the-art lookup cost decreases logarithmically as the buffer grows and absorbs small levels. Monkey's lookup cost decreases logarithmically as filter memory grows and deeper levels gain filters. Monkey removes an additive $O(\log_T E)$ term because Bloom filters depend on entry count rather than entry size. It therefore dominates for every dataset and tuning.

**Limiting Behavior.** As $T$ approaches $T_{\mathrm{lim}}$, the number of levels approaches one. Monkey and the state of the art both converge to a log under tiering and a sorted array under leveling. For all intermediate $T$, Monkey lowers lookup cost and reaches the Pareto curve.

**Main-Memory Allocation.** Let $\widehat{M}=M_{\mathrm{buffer}}+M_{\mathrm{filters}}$ be the memory, excluding fence pointers, divided between buffer and filters. Increasing buffer size at the expense of filters reveals that state-of-the-art Bloom filters can harm lookup performance over a significant range because their memory would be more useful in the buffer. Monkey removes this contention and makes lookup cost independent of buffer size as long as $M_{\mathrm{filters}}>M_{\mathrm{threshold}}/T^L$. As the buffer continues to grow and filters vanish, the two designs converge. Increasing buffer size also reduces update cost, but only logarithmically and with diminishing returns. Thus, the buffer should be as large as possible without beginning to harm lookup cost significantly—the “sweet spot” used in the next subsection.

### 4.4 Tuning Based on Workload, Hardware, and SLAs

Monkey navigates the design space by controlling merge policy, size ratio, filter memory, and buffer memory. It tunes them with respect to the dataset, workload, and storage medium, using a worst-case throughput model and an algorithm that selects the throughput-maximizing configuration.

**Modeling Throughput.** Let $r$, $v$, $q$, and $w$ be the workload proportions of zero-result point lookups, non-zero-result point lookups, range lookups, and updates, with $r+v+q+w=1$. Weighting Equations (7), (9), (11), and (10) gives average operation cost

$$
\theta=rR+vV+qQ+wW. \tag{12}
$$

If $\Omega$ is the time for one secondary-storage read, worst-case throughput is

$$
\tau=\frac{1}{\theta\Omega}. \tag{13}
$$

**Tuning Size Ratio and Merge Policy.** Merge policy and size ratio are complementary ways to navigate one trade-off continuum. Monkey linearizes this continuum and applies a divide-and-conquer search. It begins with $T=2$ and computes $\theta$. At each iteration it bisects the space and computes costs at each partition center. If either cost is lower, the search continues in that partition; otherwise, it remains at the current point and probes points twice as close. Because there are $O(T_{\mathrm{lim}})$ tunings and each iteration halves the space, the algorithm takes $O(\log_2 T_{\mathrm{lim}})$ time and runs in milliseconds. Service-level agreements can impose lookup or update upper bounds by discarding configurations that exceed them.

**Tuning Main-Memory Allocation.** Monkey uses three steps to divide $\widehat{M}$ between buffer and filters. First, since filters smaller than $M_{\mathrm{threshold}}/T^L$ yield no benefit, allocate the first $\min(\widehat{M},M_{\mathrm{threshold}}/T^L)$ bits to the buffer. Second, allocate 5% of remaining memory to the buffer and 95% to filters, approximating the sweet spot, until false-positive I/O becomes negligible. On disk, a seek is about 10 ms, so when $R$ reaches about $10^{-4}$, its average I/O latency contribution is approximately $1\ \mu$s and is insignificant beside CPU and RAM overhead. For flash, the analogous threshold is $10^{-2}$ because reads take tens to hundreds of microseconds. Third, allocate any remaining memory to the buffer to reduce update cost further.
sections.translationMarkdown:
Monkey 是一种基于 LSM 树的键值存储，能够到达并导航帕累托曲线，在任意主存预算、工作负载和存储介质下，找到查询与更新成本之间尽可能好的平衡。它为均匀随机工作负载最大化吞吐量，并为其他工作负载最大化吞吐量下界。Monkey 通过解决设计空间中的争用，并利用模型在查询成本、更新成本和主存占用之间进行最优权衡来实现这一点。

**设计旋钮。** Monkey 将影响最坏情况行为的设计要素转化为调优旋钮：（1）各层间的大小比 $T$；（2）分层或分级合并策略；（3）分配给不同层布隆过滤器的假阳性率 $p_1,\ldots,p_L$；（4）主存 $M$ 在缓冲区 $M_{\mathrm{buffer}}$ 与过滤器 $M_{\mathrm{filters}}$ 之间的分配。它可以为吞吐量联合调节这些旋钮，也可以偏向某一指标，例如平均查询或更新延迟的上界。

**最小化查询成本。** Monkey 的第一个核心要素是跨布隆过滤器的最优内存分配。查询成本与它们的假阳性率之和成正比。第 4.1 节展示如何对不同层的过滤器进行差异化调节，使总和最小，从而在任意内存预算下获得更快的读取，并将整条权衡曲线下移至帕累托曲线。

**性能预测。** Monkey 的第二个要素是预测设计选择或环境参数的变化会如何影响最坏情况性能。第 4.2 节根据设计空间旋钮推导查询与更新成本的闭式模型。例如，这些模型可以预测总主存量或其分配方式的变化将如何重新定位帕累托曲线。我们还为现有技术推导出对应模型，并证明 Monkey 占优。

**自动调优。** Monkey 的第三个要素是整体自调优，以最大化最坏情况吞吐量。第 4.3 节利用渐近分析映射设计空间，并推导在缓冲区与过滤器之间分配内存的规则。第 4.4 节根据查询和更新成本、工作负载比例以及持久化存储的读写成本，对最坏情况吞吐量建模。算法随后在帕累托曲线上搜索使最坏情况吞吐量最大的平衡点。

虽然我们将 Monkey 表述为一种键值存储，但其结果适用于任何使用 LSM 树的应用。

### 4.1 最小化查询成本

下面说明 Monkey 如何最小化最坏情况查询成本。我们首先关注零结果查询，因为它产生最大的纯 I/O 开销。最小化其最坏情况成本可提供稳健性能，后续实验也表明，非零结果查询和倾斜工作负载同样会获得显著收益。令 $R$ 表示零结果点查询的期望最坏情况 I/O 成本。我们先证明 $R$ 是所有布隆过滤器 FPR 之和，再在过滤器总内存 $M_{\mathrm{filters}}$ 的约束下调节这些 FPR，使其总和最小。本节假设条目大小 $E$ 固定；原论文附录 C 给出了适用于可变条目大小的迭代优化算法。

**建模平均最坏情况查询成本。** 零结果查询所探测游程数的期望值等于各游程过滤器 FPR 之和。在分层策略下，每层最多一个游程。在分级策略下，每层最多有 $T-1$ 个游程；第 $T$ 个游程到达时会触发合并与下推。同一分级层中的游程大小相同，因而具有相同 FPR。因此，

$$
R=
\begin{cases}
\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{L}p_i, & \text{分层},\\[4pt]
(T-1)\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{L}p_i, & \text{分级},
\end{cases}
\qquad 0<p_i\le 1. \tag{3}
$$

**建模主存占用。** 将式（2）变形得

$$
\mathrm{bits}=-\mathrm{entries}\cdot\frac{\ln(\mathrm{FPR})}{\ln(2)^2}.
$$

该式适用于任意数量、具有相同 FPR 的过滤器，因此既覆盖分层策略下每层一个过滤器，也覆盖分级策略下每层 $T-1$ 个过滤器。最后一层最多包含 $N(T-1)/T$ 个条目；第 $i$ 层最多包含 $N(T-1)/(T\cdot T^{L-i})$ 个条目。对各层求和得到

$$
M_{\mathrm{filters}}=-\frac{N}{\ln(2)^2}\cdot\frac{T-1}{T}\sum_{i=1}^{L}\frac{\ln(p_i)}{T^{L-i}}. \tag{4}
$$

**使用 Monkey 最小化查询成本。** 式（3）和式（4）可计算任意 $p_1,\ldots,p_L$ 分配方案的查询成本与过滤器占用。为了在固定 $M_{\mathrm{filters}}$ 下最小化 $R$，论文首先求解等价的逆问题：在指定 $R$ 下最小化 $M_{\mathrm{filters}}$。应用拉格朗日乘子法得到以下最优分配，其中 $L=L_{\mathrm{filtered}}+L_{\mathrm{unfiltered}}$：

$$
p_i=
\begin{cases}
1, & i>L_{\mathrm{filtered}},\\[3pt]
\displaystyle\frac{(R-L_{\mathrm{unfiltered}})(T-1)}{T^{L_{\mathrm{filtered}}+1-i}}, & \text{其他情况},
\end{cases}
$$

用于分层策略，其中 $0<R\le L$，且
$L_{\mathrm{filtered}}=L-\max(0,\lfloor R-1\rfloor)$；以及

$$
p_i=
\begin{cases}
1, & i>L_{\mathrm{filtered}},\\[3pt]
\displaystyle\frac{(R-L_{\mathrm{unfiltered}}(T-1))(T-1)}{T^{L_{\mathrm{filtered}}+1-i}}, & \text{其他情况},
\end{cases}
$$

用于分级策略，其中 $0<R\le L(T-1)$，且
$L_{\mathrm{filtered}}=L-\max(0,\lfloor(R-1)/(T-1)\rfloor)$［式（5）和式（6）］。

第 $i$ 层的最优 FPR 是第 $i-1$ 层 FPR 的 $T$ 倍，即与该层元素数成正比。无论游程大小如何，探测游程的 I/O 成本都相同；但深层条目数按指数增长，要获得较低 FPR 就需要多得多的内存。因此，应当为较小层分配更高的每元素位数，从而获得更低的 FPR。现有存储却为所有层分配相同 FPR。

随着允许的查询成本 $R$ 上升，所需过滤器内存减少。最深层过滤器的最优 FPR 达到 1 后会消失。为了以最小内存满足给定的 $R$，Monkey 选择最佳的有过滤器层数，并令每个有过滤器层的 FPR 与其容量成正比。

### 4.2 预测查询与更新成本

下面将 LSM 树设计空间映射为闭式查询与更新成本模型，并从分析上证明 Monkey 优于现有技术。

**零结果查询成本（$R$）。** 将最优 FPR 代入式（4）并变形，得到

$$
R=R_{\mathrm{filtered}}+R_{\mathrm{unfiltered}}, \tag{7}
$$

其中

$$
R_{\mathrm{filtered}}=
\begin{cases}
\displaystyle\frac{T^{T/(T-1)}}{T-1}\,e^{-\frac{M_{\mathrm{filters}}}{N}\ln(2)^2\,T^{L_{\mathrm{unfiltered}}}}, & \text{分层},\\[7pt]
\displaystyle T^{T/(T-1)}e^{-\frac{M_{\mathrm{filters}}}{N}\ln(2)^2\,T^{L_{\mathrm{unfiltered}}}}, & \text{分级},
\end{cases}
$$

且

$$
R_{\mathrm{unfiltered}}=
\begin{cases}
L_{\mathrm{unfiltered}}, & \text{分层},\\
L_{\mathrm{unfiltered}}(T-1), & \text{分级}.
\end{cases}
$$

两个加法项分别是在有过滤器层和无过滤器层中探测游程数的期望。当 $T=2$ 时，分级与分层相同，两套表达式一致。无过滤器层数为

$$
L_{\mathrm{unfiltered}}=
\begin{cases}
0, & M_{\mathrm{threshold}}\le M_{\mathrm{filters}},\\[3pt]
\left\lceil\log_T\left(\frac{M_{\mathrm{threshold}}}{M_{\mathrm{filters}}}\right)\right\rceil,
& \frac{M_{\mathrm{threshold}}}{T^L}\le M_{\mathrm{filters}}\le M_{\mathrm{threshold}},\\[7pt]
L, & 0\le M_{\mathrm{filters}}\le \frac{M_{\mathrm{threshold}}}{T^L},
\end{cases} \tag{8}
$$

其中

$$
M_{\mathrm{threshold}}=\frac{N}{\ln(2)^2}\cdot\frac{\ln(T)}{T-1}.
$$

该阈值是最后一层过滤器的 FPR 达到 1 时的内存量。

**非零结果查询成本（$V$）。** 在目标键位于最老游程的最坏情况下，从 $R$ 中减去最老过滤器的 FPR $p_L$，再加一次读取匹配页的 I/O：

$$
V=R-p_L+1. \tag{9}
$$

**更新成本（$W$）。** 假设每 $N$ 次应用写入中，一个条目至多更新一次，因此在到达最大层之前不会有条目被消除。一个条目在分级策略下每层约参与 $(T-1)/T$ 次合并，在分层策略下约参与 $(T-1)/2$ 次合并。乘以 $L$，再除以每次写 I/O 移动的 $B$ 个条目，并乘以 $(1+\phi)$，以计入读取源游程以及持久化存储的写/读成本比 $\phi$：

$$
W=
\begin{cases}
\displaystyle\frac{L}{B}\cdot\frac{T-1}{2}\cdot(1+\phi), & \text{分层},\\[7pt]
\displaystyle\frac{L}{B}\cdot\frac{T-1}{T}\cdot(1+\phi), & \text{分级}.
\end{cases} \tag{10}
$$

当 $T=2$ 时，两者相同。

**范围查询成本（$Q$）。** 范围查询在分层策略下执行 $L$ 次磁盘寻道，在分级策略下执行 $L(T-1)$ 次，随后进行顺序扫描。若查询范围包含全部条目的比例为 $s$，累计扫描量为 $sN/B$ 页：

$$
Q=
\begin{cases}
\displaystyle s\frac{N}{B}+L, & \text{分层},\\[6pt]
\displaystyle s\frac{N}{B}+L(T-1), & \text{分级}.
\end{cases} \tag{11}
$$

**对现有技术建模。** $V$、$W$ 和 $Q$ 的模型不变，因为 Monkey 的核心过滤器分配并未改变这些操作。令式（3）中的所有 $p_i$ 相等，即可得到现有技术的零结果模型；完整推导见原论文附录 E。

**Monkey 优于现有设计。** 将 Monkey 模型与现有技术模型比较可知，对任意 $M_{\mathrm{filters}}$，Monkey 的查询成本都更低，因为它以最优方式分配可用内存。当 $M_{\mathrm{filters}}$ 趋近于零时，两者都退化为无过滤器的树并相互收敛。在分级策略下，每层有 $T-1$ 个游程，因此两条查询成本曲线都沿纵轴放大 $T-1$ 倍。

### 4.3 可扩展性与可调优性

渐近分析表明，Monkey 随数据量扩展得更好，并消除了查询成本对缓冲区大小的依赖。

**复杂度分析。** 最坏情况查询复杂度取决于 $M_{\mathrm{filters}}$ 高于还是低于 $M_{\mathrm{threshold}}$。高于阈值时，所有层都有过滤器，所以 $R_{\mathrm{unfiltered}}=0$，$R_{\mathrm{filtered}}$ 占主导。将 $M_{\mathrm{threshold}}$ 代入式（7）可知，分层策略下 $R_{\mathrm{filtered}}$ 至多为 $O(1)$，分级策略下至多为 $O(T)$。随着 $N$ 相对过滤器内存增长，$M_{\mathrm{filters}}$ 最终会降至阈值以下；此时 $R_{\mathrm{unfiltered}}$ 变为非零并占主导，从至少 $O(1)$ 或 $O(T)$ 增长到 $O(L)$ 或 $O(LT)$。

条件 $M_{\mathrm{filters}}>M_{\mathrm{threshold}}$ 等价于

$$
\frac{M_{\mathrm{filters}}}{N}>\frac{1}{\ln(2)^2}\cdot\frac{\ln(T)}{T-1}.
$$

右侧最大为每元素 1.44 位，在 $T=2$ 时取得。现代存储通常采用每元素 10 位，因此在实践中，Monkey 的查询复杂度一般为 $O(R_{\mathrm{filtered}})$。

**与现有技术比较。** 当 $M_{\mathrm{filters}}\ge M_{\mathrm{threshold}}$ 时，Monkey 对两种策略都从查询复杂度中消除了一个 $O(L)$ 因子。由于

$$
L=O\left(\log_T\left(\frac{N\cdot E}{M_{\mathrm{buffer}}}\right)\right),
$$

Monkey 的查询成本在渐近意义上与层数无关。越浅层的 FPR 按几何级数下降，因此过滤器探测总成本的期望收敛于常数。这带来三项优势：

1. 如果过滤器占用随条目数扩展，使 $M_{\mathrm{filters}}/N$ 保持不变，则 Monkey 的查询成本不变，而现有技术按对数增长。因此，Monkey 的优势随数据集增大而扩大。
2. 查询成本与条目大小无关，不会因数据集中的条目更大而上升。
3. 查询成本与缓冲区大小无关，因而简化了内存调优：无需为优化查询性能而精细平衡缓冲区和过滤器。

当 $M_{\mathrm{filters}}\le M_{\mathrm{threshold}}$ 时，随着缓冲区增大并吸收小层，现有技术的查询成本按对数下降。Monkey 中，随着过滤器内存增大并覆盖更多深层，查询成本按对数下降。由于布隆过滤器取决于条目数而非条目大小，Monkey 消除了一个加性的 $O(\log_T E)$ 项，因此对所有数据集和调优方案都占优。

**极限行为。** 当 $T$ 接近 $T_{\mathrm{lim}}$ 时，层数接近 1。Monkey 与现有技术都会在分级策略下收敛为日志，在分层策略下收敛为有序数组。对于所有中间的 $T$，Monkey 都会降低查询成本并到达帕累托曲线。

**主存分配。** 令 $\widehat{M}=M_{\mathrm{buffer}}+M_{\mathrm{filters}}$ 表示在缓冲区和过滤器之间划分的内存，不包括围栏指针。以牺牲过滤器为代价增大缓冲区可揭示：在相当大的一段范围内，现有技术的布隆过滤器反而损害查询性能，因为其内存若用于缓冲区会更有价值。只要 $M_{\mathrm{filters}}>M_{\mathrm{threshold}}/T^L$，Monkey 就能消除这种争用，并使查询成本与缓冲区大小无关。当缓冲区继续增大、过滤器消失时，两种设计重新收敛。增大缓冲区也会降低更新成本，但仅按对数下降且收益递减。因此，应在不显著损害查询成本的前提下尽量增大缓冲区；这就是下一小节使用的“甜点位置”。

### 4.4 根据工作负载、硬件与 SLA 调优

Monkey 通过控制合并策略、大小比、过滤器内存和缓冲区内存来导航设计空间。它根据数据集、工作负载和存储介质对这些参数进行调节，使用最坏情况吞吐量模型以及选择吞吐量最大配置的算法。

**建模吞吐量。** 令 $r$、$v$、$q$、$w$ 分别表示零结果点查询、非零结果点查询、范围查询和更新在工作负载中的比例，且 $r+v+q+w=1$。对式（7）、式（9）、式（11）和式（10）加权，得到平均操作成本

$$
\theta=rR+vV+qQ+wW. \tag{12}
$$

若 $\Omega$ 为一次辅助存储读取所需时间，则最坏情况吞吐量为

$$
\tau=\frac{1}{\theta\Omega}. \tag{13}
$$

**调节大小比与合并策略。** 合并策略和大小比是导航同一权衡连续体的互补方式。Monkey 将连续体线性化，并使用分治搜索。算法从 $T=2$ 开始并计算 $\theta$。每轮把空间二分，并计算各分区中心的成本。若其中任一成本更低，下一轮就在该分区继续；否则保留当前位置，并探测距离缩短一半的点。由于共有 $O(T_{\mathrm{lim}})$ 种调优方案，而每轮将空间减半，算法时间复杂度为 $O(\log_2 T_{\mathrm{lim}})$，实际运行只需数毫秒。服务级别协议可施加查询或更新上界，方法是丢弃超过上界的配置。

**调节主存分配。** Monkey 分三步在缓冲区与过滤器之间划分 $\widehat{M}$。第一，小于 $M_{\mathrm{threshold}}/T^L$ 的过滤器无法带来收益，因此先把 $\min(\widehat{M},M_{\mathrm{threshold}}/T^L)$ 位分配给缓冲区。第二，将其余内存的 5% 分配给缓冲区、95% 分配给过滤器，以近似甜点位置，直到假阳性 I/O 可以忽略。磁盘寻道约为 10 ms，因此当 $R$ 达到约 $10^{-4}$ 时，其平均 I/O 延迟贡献约为 $1\ \mu$s，相比 CPU 与 RAM 开销已不显著。闪存读取需要数十至数百微秒，对应阈值为 $10^{-2}$。第三，将剩余内存全部分配给缓冲区，以进一步降低更新成本。

sections.title: 5. Experimental Analysis
sections.originalMarkdown:
We evaluate Monkey against state-of-the-art designs. We first show that its Bloom-filter tuning significantly reduces lookup cost across the design space and across workloads. We then demonstrate its ability to navigate the space and select the throughput-maximizing design for an application workload.

**Experimental Setup.** Experiments use a machine with a 500 GB, 7200 RPM disk, 32 GB of DDR4 main memory, and four 2.7 GHz cores with 8 MB of L3 cache. It runs 64-bit Ubuntu 16.04 LTS on an ext4 partition with journaling disabled.

**Implementation.** We implemented Monkey on LevelDB, a widely used and representative LSM-tree store. LevelDB occupies a fixed point in the design space: it supports only leveling, hard-codes its size ratio, and assigns the same FPR to every level. We added tiering, variable size ratios, and optimal level-specific FPRs using the algorithm from Appendix C of the original paper. For an apples-to-apples comparison, Monkey differs from LevelDB only in filter allocation; all other parameters use identical defaults. The default size ratio is 2, where leveling and tiering behave identically; the buffer is 1 MB; and filter memory is 5 bits per element overall, though Monkey distributes these bits differently among levels. We vary one parameter at a time. As in recent RocksDB versions, we implemented direct I/O to control the memory budget. The main-paper experiments disable both systems' block caches, representing the worst case of insufficient cache memory and isolating the LSM-tree structure. The original paper's Appendix F shows that Monkey retains its advantage when a block cache is available.

**Default Setup.** The database begins empty. We insert 1 GB of 1 KB key-value entries, uniformly distributed over key space and inserted in random order. We then issue 16,000 uniformly distributed zero-result point lookups. Variants use other data sizes and query workloads.

**Metrics.** Each experimental trial, including load and queries, is repeated three times. We measure average lookup latency and I/Os per lookup. Error bars represent one standard deviation of lookup latency across trials.

**Monkey Scales Better with Data Volume.** Repeating the default setup with increasing numbers of entries shows that LevelDB lookup latency rises logarithmically with the number of entries, as predicted in Section 4.3. More entries produce more levels, and LevelDB lookup cost is proportional to level count. Monkey maintains stable latency because shallower levels receive exponentially lower FPRs, causing average I/O cost to converge to a constant independent of level count. Monkey improves on LevelDB by as much as 80%, and its margin grows with the dataset. Because queries target absent keys, filters prevent most I/Os and the average can be below one I/O per lookup. Keeping entry count fixed while increasing entry size produces the same result for the same reasons.

**Monkey Needs Less Main Memory.** Repeating the default setup with different filter bits-per-entry shows that Monkey can match LevelDB with substantially less memory. At zero bits per entry, both systems have no filters and perform identically. As the memory budget grows, Monkey lowers lookup latency rapidly. Eventually both sets of filters become so accurate that I/O approaches zero and the curves converge. Except for the zero-memory extreme, Monkey matches LevelDB with a smaller footprint—up to about 60% smaller in this experiment. Its asymptotic gain implies that the advantage grows with entry count.

**Monkey Improves Lookup Cost for Different Workloads.** We evaluate non-zero-result lookups under varying temporal locality. Let $c\in[0,1]$ be a coefficient such that the most recently updated $c$ fraction of entries receives $(1-c)$ of lookups. At $c=0.5$, lookups are uniform; above 0.5 they favor recent entries, and below 0.5 they favor older entries.

For both systems, each successful lookup performs at least one I/O to fetch the target. Latency above one disk seek comes from false positives. Both systems are largely insensitive to temporal locality because recent entries occupy shallow, exponentially smaller levels, so even a lookup among the newest 10% still probes most levels on average. LevelDB latency falls slightly with increasing locality because a lookup terminates after traversing fewer levels, producing fewer false positives. Monkey is even less sensitive because every level except the last has a much lower FPR. Traversing fewer shallow levels changes little because false positives there are already rare. Monkey improves non-zero-result lookup latency by as much as 30% across the locality range.

**Monkey Reaches the Pareto Curve.** Repeating the setup under different merge policies and size ratios, and plotting average lookup latency against update latency, shows that Monkey achieves significantly lower lookup cost for every configuration because of its filter tuning. It shifts the trade-off curve to the Pareto frontier, improving lookup cost by as much as 60%. By choosing a more update-friendly policy and size ratio, that gain can instead be traded for as much as a 70% update-cost improvement.

**Monkey Navigates the Design Space to Maximize Throughput.** We vary the zero-result-lookup/update ratio from 10% to 90% and compare LevelDB with two Monkey instances. Fixed Monkey uses the default configuration with $T=2$. Navigable Monkey selects the merge policy, size ratio, and corresponding optimal filter allocation that maximize throughput. LevelDB and Fixed Monkey both retain $T=2$. Navigable Monkey's throughput curve is bell-shaped because workloads dominated by one operation type permit a specialized configuration that handles most operations well. Fixed Monkey significantly improves on LevelDB, and Navigable Monkey significantly improves on Fixed Monkey. By reaching the Pareto curve and navigating the design space, Navigable Monkey more than doubles throughput relative to LevelDB.
sections.translationMarkdown:
我们将 Monkey 与最先进设计进行比较。首先证明其布隆过滤器调优能在整个设计空间和多种工作负载下降低查询成本；随后展示它导航设计空间并为应用工作负载选择吞吐量最大设计的能力。

**实验环境。** 实验机器配备一块 500 GB、7200 RPM 磁盘，32 GB DDR4 主存，以及四个 2.7 GHz 核心和 8 MB L3 缓存。系统为 64 位 Ubuntu 16.04 LTS，使用关闭日志功能的 ext4 分区。

**实现。** 我们在广泛使用、具有代表性的 LSM 树存储 LevelDB 上实现 Monkey。LevelDB 处于设计空间中的一个固定点：只支持分层策略，硬编码大小比，并为每层分配相同 FPR。我们增加了分级策略、可变大小比，以及利用原论文附录 C 算法计算的逐层最优 FPR。为了公平比较，Monkey 与 LevelDB 仅在过滤器分配上不同，其他参数均采用相同默认值。默认大小比为 2，此时分层与分级行为相同；缓冲区为 1 MB；过滤器总内存为每元素 5 位，但 Monkey 会在各层之间以不同方式分配这些位。实验每次只改变一个参数。与近期 RocksDB 版本类似，我们实现了直接 I/O，以便控制内存预算。论文正文中的实验关闭了两个系统的块缓存，这代表缓存内存不足的最坏情况，并隔离出纯粹的 LSM 树结构。原论文附录 F 表明，存在块缓存时 Monkey 仍保有优势。

**默认设置。** 数据库初始为空。我们插入 1 GB 的键值条目，每个条目 1 KB；条目均匀分布在键空间中，并以随机顺序插入。随后执行 16,000 次均匀分布的零结果点查询。实验变体使用其他数据规模和查询工作负载。

**指标。** 每次实验试验（包括加载与查询）重复三次。我们测量平均查询延迟和每次查询的 I/O 数。误差条表示各次试验查询延迟的一个标准差。

**Monkey 随数据量扩展得更好。** 使用不断增加的条目数重复默认设置后可见，LevelDB 的查询延迟随条目数按对数增长，与第 4.3 节的预测一致。条目越多，层数越多，而 LevelDB 查询成本与层数成正比。Monkey 则保持稳定延迟，因为它为较浅层分配按指数降低的 FPR，使平均 I/O 成本收敛于与层数无关的常数。Monkey 相比 LevelDB 最多改善 80%，且优势随数据集增大而扩大。由于查询目标是不存在的键，过滤器阻止了大多数 I/O，平均每次查询的 I/O 可以小于一次。保持条目数不变而增大条目大小，会因相同原因得到相同结果。

**Monkey 需要更少主存。** 使用不同的过滤器每元素位数重复默认设置，结果表明 Monkey 能以显著更少的内存达到 LevelDB 的性能。每元素为零位时，两个系统都没有过滤器，性能相同。随着内存预算增大，Monkey 会迅速降低查询延迟。最终，两者的过滤器都变得十分精确，I/O 接近零，曲线相互收敛。除零内存这一极端情况外，Monkey 总能以更小占用匹配 LevelDB；本实验中最多可小约 60%。其渐近收益意味着该优势会随条目数增加而扩大。

**Monkey 改善不同工作负载下的查询成本。** 我们在不同时间局部性下评估非零结果查询。令 $c\in[0,1]$ 为系数，使最近更新的 $c$ 比例条目获得 $(1-c)$ 比例的查询。当 $c=0.5$ 时，查询均匀分布；高于 0.5 时偏向近期条目，低于 0.5 时偏向较老条目。

对两个系统而言，每次成功查询至少执行一次 I/O 以取得目标。超过一次磁盘寻道的延迟来自假阳性。两者对时间局部性都基本不敏感，因为近期条目位于浅层，而浅层容量按指数变小，所以即使查询最新的 10% 条目，平均仍需探测大多数层。随着局部性增强，LevelDB 延迟略有下降，因为查询在遍历较少层后终止，产生的假阳性也更少。Monkey 更不敏感，因为除最后一层外，所有层的 FPR 都低得多。遍历较少的浅层几乎不会改变成本，因为这些层原本就很少出现假阳性。在整个局部性范围内，Monkey 最多将非零结果查询延迟改善 30%。

**Monkey 到达帕累托曲线。** 在不同合并策略和大小比下重复实验，并将平均查询延迟与更新延迟相对绘制后可见，得益于过滤器调优，Monkey 在每种配置下的查询成本都显著更低。它把权衡曲线下移到帕累托前沿，最多将查询成本改善 60%。通过选择更偏向更新的策略与大小比，也可以把这项收益换成最多 70% 的更新成本改善。

**Monkey 导航设计空间以最大化吞吐量。** 我们将零结果查询/更新比从 10% 变化到 90%，并将 LevelDB 与两个 Monkey 实例比较。Fixed Monkey 使用 $T=2$ 的默认配置。Navigable Monkey 选择使吞吐量最大的合并策略、大小比及相应的最优过滤器分配。LevelDB 和 Fixed Monkey 均保持 $T=2$。Navigable Monkey 的吞吐量曲线呈钟形，因为当工作负载由某一种操作主导时，可以采用专门配置，使大部分操作得到良好处理。Fixed Monkey 显著优于 LevelDB，Navigable Monkey 又显著优于 Fixed Monkey。通过到达帕累托曲线并导航设计空间，Navigable Monkey 的吞吐量超过 LevelDB 的两倍。

sections.title: 6. Related Work
sections.originalMarkdown:
**LSM-Based Key-Value Stores.** Like state-of-the-art LSM-tree key-value stores [4, 12, 15, 18, 19, 21, 29, 34], Monkey uses in-memory Bloom filters to let lookups probabilistically skip runs that do not contain the target key. Existing stores assign the same FPR to every filter regardless of run size. Yet worst-case lookup cost is proportional to the sum of all FPRs, and equal FPRs do not minimize this sum. Monkey minimizes it by setting each filter's FPR proportional to the number of entries in its run; shallower runs therefore receive exponentially lower FPRs. This reduces the asymptotic complexity of worst-case lookup cost and, in the experiments, cuts lookup latency by 50%–80% without a trade-off elsewhere.

**Holistic Tuning.** Existing LSM-tree stores do not make lookup cost, update cost, and memory footprint easy to trade. LevelDB [19] hard-codes a size ratio of 10 under leveled compaction. RocksDB [15] and LSM-trie [35] expose only leveled and tiered compaction, respectively, and assign a fixed number of bits per element to every filter. WiredTiger [34] supports dynamic size ratios beginning at 15 but defaults to 16 bits per element for all filters. Because the balance depends on interdependent tuning and environmental parameters, tuning is effectively trial and error requiring experienced engineers.

Monkey is a step toward automation. It exposes the knobs that govern worst-case lookup cost, update cost, and memory footprint, and uses closed-form models to optimize throughput and answer what-if questions about environmental changes. Complementary work [23] numerically estimates update cost for a leveled LSM-tree under skewed updates. Monkey goes further by modeling lookup and update costs under both policies and enabling holistic tuning across the entire space.

**De-amortizing Merge Operations.** LSM-tree stores spread merge work over time to stabilize performance. Some pace merges directly against application updates [9, 22, 29]. Others partition runs into Sorted String Tables (SSTables) and merge one file at a time with overlapping files in the next level [4, 12, 15, 18, 19, 21, 29, 34]. Other work proposes merging runs on dedicated servers [1]. Monkey concerns the total merge work rather than its scheduling and can therefore be combined with any of these techniques.

**Reducing Merge Overheads.** WiscKey [26] reduces copied data by separating values from keys and storing values in a log. It is compatible with Monkey's core design, but the cost models would need to account for merging keys alone and accessing the log during lookups. VT-tree [30] avoids merging nonoverlapping run portions. Major key-value stores use a related optimization: an SSTable is simply moved to the next level when it overlaps no SSTable there [4, 12, 15, 19, 21, 29]. Monkey's LevelDB implementation benefits from this technique.

**In-Memory Stores.** Redis [28] and Memcached [17] store application data in main memory rather than persistent secondary storage. This paper maps the design space of persistent key-value stores and is orthogonal to in-memory systems. Similar trade-offs may exist in memory for minimizing cache misses, so an analogous mapping of in-memory design spaces may be useful.
sections.translationMarkdown:
**基于 LSM 的键值存储。** 与最先进的 LSM 树键值存储 [4, 12, 15, 18, 19, 21, 29, 34] 一样，Monkey 使用内存中的布隆过滤器，使查询能够以概率方式跳过不含目标键的游程。现有存储不考虑游程大小，为每个过滤器分配相同 FPR。然而，最坏情况查询成本与全部 FPR 之和成正比，而相等 FPR 并不能使总和最小。Monkey 令每个过滤器的 FPR 与相应游程的条目数成正比，从而最小化该总和；因此，较浅游程的 FPR 按指数降低。这降低了最坏情况查询成本的渐近复杂度，并在实验中将查询延迟降低 50%–80%，且不牺牲其他方面。

**整体调优。** 现有 LSM 树存储不容易在查询成本、更新成本和内存占用之间进行权衡。LevelDB [19] 在分层压缩下硬编码大小比 10。RocksDB [15] 和 LSM-trie [35] 分别只提供分层和分级压缩，并为每个过滤器分配固定的每元素位数。WiredTiger [34] 支持从 15 起始的动态大小比，但默认仍为所有过滤器分配每元素 16 位。由于平衡取决于相互依赖的调优参数和环境参数，调优实际上成为一种依赖资深工程师直觉与经验的试错过程。

Monkey 是迈向自动化的一步。它暴露控制最坏情况查询成本、更新成本和内存占用的旋钮，并使用闭式模型优化吞吐量，回答环境变化方面的假设性问题。互补工作 [23] 以数值方法估计更新倾斜时分层 LSM 树的更新成本。Monkey 更进一步，对两种策略下的查询与更新成本都进行建模，从而在整个空间中实现整体调优。

**合并操作的去摊销。** LSM 树存储会把合并工作分散到一段时间内，以稳定性能。一些系统按照应用更新直接调度合并 [9, 22, 29]；另一些系统把游程划分为有序字符串表（Sorted String Table，SSTable），每次将一个文件与下一层中键范围相交的文件集合合并 [4, 12, 15, 18, 19, 21, 29, 34]；还有工作提出在专用服务器上合并游程 [1]。Monkey 关注合并的总工作量，而非工作调度方式，因此可以与其中任何技术结合。

**降低合并开销。** WiscKey [26] 将值与键分离，并把值存入日志，从而减少复制数据量。它与 Monkey 的核心设计兼容，但成本模型需要计入只合并键以及查询期间访问日志。VT-tree [30] 避免合并游程中互不重叠的部分。主要键值存储采用了一种相关优化：若一个 SSTable 与下一层的任何 SSTable 都不重叠，便直接将它移动到下一层 [4, 12, 15, 19, 21, 29]。Monkey 的 LevelDB 实现利用了这项技术。

**内存存储。** Redis [28] 和 Memcached [17] 将应用数据保存在主存中，而不是持久化辅助存储中。本文映射持久化键值存储的设计空间，与内存系统正交。为减少缓存未命中，内存环境中可能存在类似权衡，因此对内存设计空间进行对应映射或许同样有用。

sections.title: 7. Conclusion
sections.originalMarkdown: LSM-tree key-value stores exhibit an intrinsic trade-off among lookup cost, update cost, and main-memory footprint, but existing designs strike a suboptimal and difficult-to-tune balance. Monkey reaches the Pareto performance curve by allocating memory among Bloom filters to minimize worst-case lookup cost. It uses a closed-form model to navigate the design space and find the holistic tuning that maximizes throughput under a given memory budget, application workload, and storage medium.
sections.translationMarkdown: LSM 树键值存储在查询成本、更新成本和主存占用之间存在内在权衡，但现有设计取得的是次优且难以调节的平衡。Monkey 在布隆过滤器之间分配内存，使最坏情况查询成本最小，从而到达帕累托性能曲线。它使用闭式模型导航设计空间，在给定内存预算、应用工作负载和存储介质下，找到使吞吐量最大的整体调优方案。
